title: Discovering Character and Crafting Your Own Autobiography
explanation_chunks: When you 'infer' someone's personality from their writing, it means you're figuring out what kind of person they are based on their actions, thoughts, and how they describe things, even if they don't directly tell you.
It's really important to use 'evidence' to support your ideas about a character's personality. This means picking exact sentences or phrases from the text that prove your point.

To make your own writing even better, try to include lots of specific details and choose 'powerful verbs'. These verbs make your sentences stronger and help the reader imagine what's happening more clearly.

When you're writing your own autobiography, remember to include 'facts and opinions' (what happened and how you felt), 'precise verbs' and 'adverbial phrases of time' (to show exactly when and how things happened), and a 'reflective comment' at the end.
examples: Inferring personality: If Jane Goodall 'recognised him at once. It was the handsome male with the dense white beard,' you could infer she is 'observant' because she notices details.
Powerful verbs: Instead of 'The monkey moved', try 'The monkey darted' or 'The monkey bounded'.

Punctuation: Make sure to use commas to separate items in a list, semi-colons to link related sentences, and colons to introduce lists or explanations, along with all the usual full stops, question marks, and exclamation marks.
key_points: You can infer a writer's personality from their autobiography by analyzing their words and actions.
Always use quotations as evidence to support your opinions about a text.
Improve your writing by using more details and stronger, more precise verbs.
A good autobiography chapter includes facts, opinions, precise language, and a reflective comment.
Using a variety of punctuation correctly makes your writing easy to understand.

vocabulary.definition: To figure something out based on clues, rather than being told directly.
Able to wait calmly without getting annoyed.
Ready to face danger or pain without showing fear.
Not feeling or showing excitement, anger, or nervousness.
Facts or information that show something is true.
To judge the quality or importance of something.
Words that describe an action very exactly.
Groups of words that tell when something happens.
A thought or opinion shared by the writer, looking back at an event.

explain_again: This page is like being a detective for stories! We learn to guess what a person is like from their words, and always use proof. When you write your own story, make sure to use exciting words and lots of details, and don't forget your full stops and commas!
fun_analogy: Inferring someone's personality is like piecing together clues in a mystery. Each action, each word they choose, is a little puzzle piece that helps you build a picture of who they really are, without them ever having to say 'I am kind'!
